description: In-depth analysis of sport psychology interrelationships: flow state, the choking mechanism, team cohesion dynamics, measurement of mental toughness, and the application of Self-Determination Theory in sport.

questions.text: What is the 'flow' state in Csikszentmihalyi's theory, and when is it most likely to occur?
questions.explanation: Flow is the psychological state of optimal performance: complete involvement, intrinsic motivation, and loss of the sense of time. It is most likely to occur when the difficulty of the task and the athlete's skill level are in balance — if the challenge exceeds ability, anxiety results; if it falls short, boredom develops.

options.text: Exclusively during easy tasks, when there is no stress
When the difficulty of the task and the athlete's skill level are in balance
Exclusively in team sports; it does not occur in individual sport
Flow is determined exclusively by genetic factors

questions.text: What is the psychological mechanism behind 'choking under pressure'?
questions.explanation: The most accepted explanation for choking is the explicit monitoring hypothesis: under pressure, the athlete consciously attends to automated movements (which normally run without conscious awareness), disrupting their automated execution — analogous to when an expert walker begins to consciously think about each step.

options.text: The athlete becomes physically exhausted at the decisive moment of competition
Explicit monitoring: the athlete consciously attends to automated movements, disrupting their execution
Choking occurs only in weak athletes
Pressure directly reduces muscular strength

questions.text: According to Self-Determination Theory (SDT), external rewards always increase athletes' motivation.
questions.explanation: False. According to SDT, external rewards (when perceived as controlling) can reduce intrinsic motivation (crowding-out effect). Satisfying the basic needs of competence, autonomy, and relatedness leads to sustainable intrinsic motivation — the effect of rewards depends on whether the athlete interprets them as informational or controlling.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: What are the two dimensions of team cohesion and how do they differ in terms of sports performance?
questions.explanation: Team cohesion has two main dimensions: (1) task cohesion — the degree to which the team works together toward a shared goal, and (2) social cohesion — the closeness of personal relationships among team members. Research indicates that task cohesion correlates more strongly with team performance than social cohesion — the 'team of friends' is not always the most effective.

options.text: Cohesion has no dimensions; it is a unitary concept
Task cohesion (shared goal) and social cohesion (personal relationships) — task cohesion has a stronger effect on performance
Social cohesion always has a stronger effect on performance
Cohesion can only be influenced by the coach

questions.text: Which dimension of mental toughness (the 4C model) is most amenable to development through training?
questions.explanation: The 4C model (Control, Commitment, Challenge, Confidence) holds that 'Commitment' is the most developable dimension through training: goal-setting, adherence to plans, and accountability can be structurally improved — in contrast to certain aspects of the 'Control' dimension, which are temperament-dependent.

options.text: Confidence cannot be developed
Commitment — goal-setting, plan adherence, and accountability can be improved through structured development
Mental toughness cannot be developed at all
Only the Challenge dimension can be developed

questions.text: What is the mechanism of action of imagery (mental rehearsal) on sports performance?
questions.explanation: Mental imagery activates the motor cortex and cerebellum — eliciting almost the same neural pattern as actual movement execution. This reinforces motor patterns, improves tactical decision-making, and reduces competition anxiety.

options.text: Imagery serves exclusively relaxation purposes
It activates the motor cortex and cerebellum — reinforces motor patterns, improves tactical decision-making
Imagery is ineffective because it involves no physical movement
Imagery can only be applied in post-injury rehabilitation

questions.text: A pre-performance routine (pre-competition ritual) is effective because its superstitious belief reduces anxiety.
questions.explanation: False. The effectiveness of a pre-performance routine is not based on superstition but on psychological mechanisms: (1) focusing attention on relevant stimuli, (2) arousal regulation, (3) automatisation — executing routines reduces the burden of conscious processing and facilitates reaching optimal activation level.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: What is 'social loafing' and how can it be prevented in a football team?
questions.explanation: Social loafing is the reduction in individual effort that occurs when a team member feels that their individual contribution cannot be measured or does not matter. In a football team, it can be mitigated by clarifying individual performance expectations, tracking position-specific metrics, and emphasising each team member's individual accountability.

options.text: Athletes moving slowly due to fatigue
Reduced individual effort in a group setting — mitigated through individual metrics and clear accountability
The complete absence of social cohesion in the team
The coach's presence always prevents social loafing

questions.text: How can cognitive reappraisal be applied to manage competition anxiety?
questions.explanation: Cognitive reappraisal reinterprets the physiological signs of anxiety (rapid heartbeat, sweating) not as danger signals but as signs of readiness ('I am excited' vs 'I am anxious'). Research shows this is more effective than attempting to calm down, because reducing physiological arousal is difficult — whereas changing the interpretation is achievable.

options.text: Suppressing anxiety symptoms through deep breathing
Reinterpreting physiological signs of anxiety as readiness signals — more effective than forcing calmness
Achieving complete relaxation before competition
Withdrawing from the competitive situation

questions.text: As team size increases, team performance automatically improves.
questions.explanation: False. According to the Ringelmann effect, as team size grows, individual performance contribution decreases (social loafing increases). Additionally, the complexity of communication and coordination increases — the relationship between size and performance is an inverted U-shape: an optimal team size exists.

options.text: True
False
